author: times news cr
title: The Syrian Army is about to announce the liberation of Western Ghouta ¬Ľ
text:
Affiliate ‚Äď INA Local sources‚Ā£ in Damascus reported that the announcement of the liberation of Western Ghouta from Al-Nusra militants and‚Ā£ their removal from their last strongholds in the region, ‚Äćthrough an ‚Äčagreement‚Ā§ that would take them ‚Äćtowards Idlib , after ‚Äčthe Syrian‚Äć army advanced and imposed a siege on them, was‚ÄĆ imminent. The Syrian newspaper ‚ÄúAl-Watan‚ÄĚ quoted informed sources as saying that the former Al-Nusra‚ĀĘ ‚ÄúHay‚Äôat Tahrir Al-Sham‚ÄĚ, in the southwestern Damascus countryside, submitted to the conditions of ‚Ā£the Syrian army after it imposed a siege‚ÄĆ on it, and agreed‚ĀĘ to leave for Idlib within ‚Ā£48 hours, with the first batch leaving today.‚Ā§ Of them, if the ‚ÄĆagreement is not violated by them again, while the conditions of those who wish to stay are settled. ‚ĀĘThe Syrian army was able to achieve progress in ‚ĀĘthe southwestern Damascus countryside, ‚ÄĆafter violent clashes with the armed ‚Äćfactions, and took control of new points in ‚Ā§the hills and highlands governing the region and achieved progress in Mughr al-Mir, Tal Marwan,‚Äč and Beit Jinn farm, ‚Äčwhich greatly tightened the noose on the Al-Nusra Front and the allied factions. Al-Watan newspaper indicated that the leaders of the ‚Äćterrorist groups fled to Mount Hermon, specifically to the Lebanese border and‚Ā§ to Shebaa.‚ĀĘ The newspaper pointed out that the reconciliation committees began conducting negotiations with ‚Äćthe remaining group leaders ‚Ā§and Al-Nusra militants, who eventually relented and agreed to leave for Idlib ‚ÄĆwith their families, and the entire villages ‚Äčof Jabal Al-Sheikh would be ‚Äćhanded over to the Syrian army, and if that was done, the Al-Nusra file would be ‚Äćthe‚Ā£ case. The ‚Äčfactions ‚ÄĆallied with it have finished in the Western Ghouta of Damascus. In turn, activists ‚Äčconfirmed that negotiations‚Äč are taking place‚Ā£ in the southwestern Damascus countryside to reach‚Ā§ an agreement on removing Hay‚Äôat Tahrir al-Sham from the southwestern‚Ā£ Damascus countryside and transferring it ‚ĀĘto Idlib Governorate ‚ÄĆin‚Ā£ northern‚ÄĆ Syria,‚Ā£ after weeks of violent clashes between the army and‚Ā§ militants. At a time when the date of liberating Western Ghouta from Al-Nusra is approaching, ambiguity still surrounds the file of Eastern Ghouta, as opposition websites revealed a dispute regarding the exit of‚Ā£ Hay‚Äôat Tahrir ‚ÄĆAl-Sham militants from ‚ÄčEastern Ghouta. The opposition websites explained‚ĀĘ that the ongoing dispute is between the same armed factions ‚ĀĘinside ‚Ā£Eastern Ghouta, specifically between the ‚ÄúArmy of Islam‚ÄĚ and some‚Äć of the leaders of ‚ÄúHay‚Äôat Tahrir al-Sham‚ÄĚ, including ‚ÄúFaylaq al-Rahman‚ÄĚ, which refused to leave Ghouta without releasing its ‚Äúprisoners‚ÄĚ in the prisons of‚Äč the ‚ÄúArmy of Islam.‚ÄĚ . Opposition ‚ÄĆmedia ‚Äćoutlets reported that the current circumstances confirm the ‚Äčpostponement‚Ā§ of the agreement until after ‚Äčthe ‚ÄúAstana-9‚ÄĚ‚Ā§ talks, scheduled to‚ÄĆ be held in mid-January. In addition, informed sources confirmed that ‚Äča delegation from the former Nusra Front, Tahrir al-Sham, which‚Äć was besieged in the Yarmouk camp, met with a delegation from ‚ĀĘthe Syrian army, and ‚Äčan‚Ā£ agreement was made that stipulated the exit of gunmen ‚ĀĘfrom the Yarmouk camp. Source: Agencies
How might the future ‚Ā£regrouping of Al-Nusra ‚Ā£militants affect the stability of the region, particularly in Idlib? Interview between Time.news‚ĀĘ Editor‚ĀĘ and Military Analyst Dr. ‚ĀĘSamira‚ĀĘ Al-Hakim
Time.news Editor (E): Good‚ĀĘ day, Dr. Al-Hakim, and thank you for joining us to discuss the recent developments ‚Ā§in Western Ghouta. The news from‚Ā§ Damascus suggests that‚Äć the Syrian army‚Ā£ has achieved a significant‚Äč victory against Al-Nusra militants.‚Äć Can‚Ā§ you elaborate on the implications of this‚Ā£ milestone?
Dr. Samira Al-Hakim (A): ‚ĀĘ Thank you for having me.‚Ā£ Yes, the situation in Western Ghouta is quite pivotal. ‚ÄĆThe Syrian army‚Äôs ‚Ā§ability ‚Ā§to impose a‚ÄĆ siege on Al-Nusra‚Ā§ militants has not only led to their withdrawal but also highlights the strategic prowess of the military in reclaiming territory. This victory can alter the balance of power in the region and potentially pave the‚Ā£ way‚Ā§ for‚Ā£ further consolidation of government control.
E: It seems‚ÄĆ that the agreement to move Al-Nusra militants to ‚ÄĆIdlib was ‚ÄĆreached after intense conflict. What do you think ‚Äćmotivated these groups to eventually agree to such terms?
A: The ‚ÄĆsiege imposed by the Syrian‚ĀĘ army undeniably crippled their operational capabilities. After enduring violent clashes and losing key positions in ‚Äćthe southwestern Damascus‚ĀĘ countryside, it became increasingly untenable for Al-Nusra to remain. The imminent threat of complete encirclement likely compelled their leaders to negotiate rather than ‚Äčface ‚Ā§annihilation‚ÄĒa pragmatic choice‚ĀĘ under‚Äč pressure,‚ÄĆ despite their‚Ā£ past resistance.
E: The report indicates that leaders of these groups fled ‚ÄĆto Mount‚Äć Hermon‚ĀĘ and the Lebanese ‚ÄĆborder. ‚Ā£What does this‚Ā£ suggest about the future of these factions and their potential regrouping?
A: The escape of leaders to areas near the Lebanese border signifies an attempt to preserve command and ‚ÄĆcontrol. ‚ÄčThese regions historically serve as safe havens for various factions. If they can‚Ā§ re-establish order and cohesive strategy, ‚ÄĆwe may witness a ‚Ā£resurgence or reorganization, particularly‚Ā£ in Idlib, where they can exploit ‚ÄĆthe ongoing chaos. Nonetheless, their survival depends on both internal‚Äć unity and ‚ĀĘexternal support.
E: What does this mean for the civilian population remaining in these regions, particularly those in Jabal Al-Sheikh?
A: It raises crucial humanitarian concerns. As‚ÄĆ the Syrian army establishes control, the fate of civilians caught between military objectives is precarious. The reconciliation efforts and‚Äč negotiations with remaining militants could offer some respite,‚Äć but ‚Ā£the security ‚Äćsituation remains volatile. ‚ÄčThe potential for displacement increases, as families may ‚Ā£be torn between staying or leaving under duress alongside the‚Ā£ fighters.
E: The situation‚ÄĆ is undoubtedly complex. How do you view the‚Ā§ international community‚Äôs role amid these developments, especially concerning humanitarian access and the dynamics of ‚Ā£the ongoing ‚ÄĆconflict?
A: ‚ĀĘThe‚Ā§ international community has a ‚Ā£significant responsibility. While‚Äć they may express concern over military operations, tangible actions to ensure humanitarian access and protection for civilians are ‚ÄĆcritical. Furthermore, nations need to engage in diplomatic efforts to ‚Äćfacilitate stability post-conflict. However, the fragmented nature of power dynamics complicates these efforts, as various ‚ĀĘfactions and external forces vie for influence ‚ÄĆin Syria.
E: ‚Äč Thank you, Dr.‚ÄĆ Al-Hakim, for this insightful discussion. Your expertise sheds much-needed light on the unfolding ‚ÄĆsituation‚Äć in Syria and ‚Ā§its broader implications.
A: ‚ÄćIt was my pleasure. The developments in Syria are‚Ā§ of great importance, and I hope for peace ‚ÄĆand stability‚Ā§ for the affected populations. ‚ÄćThank you for having me.
